title: Ordering, correlation functions and phase transitions in molecular systems
abstract: Although the classical density functional theory (DFT) of inhomogeneous fluids was formulated more than four decades ago, its application to broken symmetry phases of molecular systems remained a challenge. Approximate free energy functionals proposed in the past failed to give accurate description of relative stability of phases, phase transitions, and of properties arising due to broken symmetry. In a DFT pair correlation functions (PCFs) play a fundamental role. While in the case of homogeneous fluids, PCFs are routinely determined using experimental, theoretical or simulation methods, determination of PCFs of broken symmetry phases remained a problem. Breaking of symmetry at the transition point gives rise a new contribution to correlation functions which may differ significantly from that of the coexisting higher symmetry phase. We review methods which have been developed in the last few years to calculate PCFs of broken symmetry phases and their inclusion in the expressions of the grand potential and the intrinsic free-energy. This leads to formulation of an exact DFT. We describe application of the theory to freezing of variety of fluids into ordered phases and transition from an ordered phase of higher symmetry to a phase of lower symmetry. Comparison of results found from different versions of DFT and simulations reveal their accuracy. A brief description of basics of statistical mechanics is included to make the article self-contained.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive review of advanced Density Functional Theory methods for describing phase transitions in molecular systems. It focuses on overcoming the limitations of previous approximations by accurately capturing how correlation functions change when a system breaks symmetry. The work demonstrates high accuracy in predicting the stability of complex phases like liquid crystals and crystalline solids.
main_problem: The challenge of accurately applying classical Density Functional Theory (DFT) to broken symmetry phases in molecular systems, specifically regarding the determination of pair correlation functions (PCFs) and the relative stability of phases.
main_result: The development of an 'exact DFT' and an Effective DFT (EDFT) approach that accounts for new contributions to correlation functions during symmetry breaking, providing accurate predictions for phase boundaries (e.g., fluid-solid, bcc-fcc) and thermodynamic stability.
method: The use of functional calculus, Legendre transformations, and integral equation theories (such as RY and ZH closures) to relate the grand potential and intrinsic free energy to density distributions and direct correlation functions.
model_or_system: Classical many-body systems of rigid or asymmetric molecules (including isotropic fluids, liquid crystals, and crystalline solids) interacting via pairwise additive potentials, such as Lennard-Jones or power-law potentials.
key_observables: One-particle density, pair correlation functions (PCFs), direct correlation functions (DCFs), order parameters (positional and orientational), and the structure factor S(q).
important_parameters: Temperature, pressure, chemical potential, molecular anisotropy, softness parameter (1/n), and the degree of particle localization (alpha).
main_assumptions: The system is treated in the classical limit with pairwise additive intermolecular potentials; specific models are limited to rigid linear molecules or specific symmetries.
figures_summary: The figures show the variation of free energy difference as a function of density and localization, phase diagrams in the softness vs. density plane, and density-temperature phase diagrams.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the basics of statistical mechanics, moves to the derivation of the grand potential and intrinsic free energy in terms of correlation functions, reviews recent developments in calculating PCFs for broken symmetry phases, and concludes with applications to various phase transitions and comparisons with simulation data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig.7.1 Comparison of some of the harmonics of the total pair correlation function in the Fourier space for the nematic phase (βa2 = 1, βa0 = 0.1, z0σ = z2σ = 1, η = 0.315, P2 = 0.63, P4 = 0.27) obtained by the analytical solution [40] (dashed line) with the results found using the numerical method (full line) for the MSA. The two curves are indistinguishable at the scale of the ﬁgure. Reproduced with permission from Ref [47]

Fig.7.2 Comparison of the harmonic coeﬃcients c221(r∗) and h221(r∗) obtained by the analytical solution (Ref. [40]) (dashed line) with the results found using the numerical method (full line) for the MSA. Parameters are the same as in Fig. 7.1. A 1/r∗tail in the harmonic coeﬃcient h221(r∗) is seen. Reproduced with permission from Ref [47].

Fig.7.3 Comparison of the structure factor curves for the nematic phase (βa2 = 1, βa0 = 0.1, z0σ = z2σ = 1, η = 0.315, P2 = 0.63, P4 = 0.27). The dashed line is obtained with the analytical solution (Ref. [40]) while the full line is obtained by our numerical method for the MSA. The two curves overlap at all values of k. Reproduced with permission from Ref [47].

Fig.7.4 Plot of the harmonic coeﬃcients of the direct pair correlation functions c220(r∗), c221(r∗), and c200(r∗) obtained by solving the PY integral equation theory (βa2 = 1, βa0 = 0.1, z0σ = z2σ = 1, η = 0.30). While the contribution of the sym- metry breaking part in c220(r∗) is small in c221(r∗) it is comparable inside the core. The harmonic coeﬃcient c200(r∗) arises due to symmetry breaking only. The symme- try conserving contribution is shown by dotted line and the broken symmetry part by dot–dashed line and the total by full line. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [47].

Fig.7.5 Harmonic coeﬃcient h221(r∗) in the director frame. Details are the same as in Fig. 7.4. Inset shows the plot of h221(r∗) with respect to 1/r∗; the dashed line shows the extrapolated part. The origin of the tail is due to orientational symmetry breaking. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [47].
